title: Classical Music Aims to Evolve and Build New Audiences
subtitle: 古典音乐如何放下身段吸引年轻观众

paragraphs.en: In 1913, classical music sparked a riot in Paris. Igor Stravinsky was introducing his revolutionary "Rite of Spring" ballet to the world, with its discordant melodies and unorthodox choreography, and the purists in the crowd expressed their disapproval loud and clear. It might have been classical music's version of the time Bob Dylan went electric at the Newport Folk Festival.
paragraphs.zh: 1913年，古典音乐在巴黎引发了一场骚乱。伊戈尔·斯特拉文斯基正向世界介绍他革命性的芭蕾舞剧《春之祭》，其旋律刺耳不协，编舞离经叛道，台下的纯粹主义者毫不掩饰地表达了他们的强烈反对。这大概就是古典音乐版的鲍勃·迪伦在纽波特民谣节上插电演出。

paragraphs.en: "The noise, fighting, and shouting in the audience got so loud," NPR's music reporter Miles Hoffman said of the Stravinsky debut, "that the choreographer had to shout out the numbers to the dancers so that they knew what they were supposed to do." It's difficult to imagine a similar disturbance occurring today within America's sacred symphony halls.
paragraphs.zh: 美国国家公共电台音乐记者迈尔斯·霍夫曼如此描述斯特拉文斯基的首演：“观众席上的噪音、打斗和喊叫声震耳欲聋，编舞不得不向舞者大声喊出节拍，好让他们知道该做什么。”今天，我们很难想象在美国神圣的交响乐大厅内会发生类似的骚乱。

paragraphs.en: In fact, it's hard to picture any kind of disruptive activity at all. A mannerly aura hangs over most classical proceedings, and many of the genre's biggest supporters would have it no other way. Today, Western audiences for classical music and opera and ballet are almost always well dressed, older, respectful, achingly silent and often very wealthy.
paragraphs.zh: 事实上，如今几乎想象不出会有任何形式的干扰行为。大多数古典音乐演出都笼罩在彬彬有礼的氛围中，而这一艺术形式的众多忠实拥趸也正乐见于此。如今，西方古典音乐、歌剧和芭蕾舞的观众几乎总是衣着得体、年纪较长、举止恭敬、静默无声，且往往家境殷实。

paragraphs.en: But as many of America's most storied "highbrow" institutions struggle financially — the Philadelphia Orchestra's much-publicized rebound from bankruptcy is just one recent example — classical music fans and theorists are wondering how the medium can weave itself into the 21st century's cultural fabric without sacrificing its integrity.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，随着美国许多久负盛名的“高雅”艺术机构陷入财务困境——费城交响乐团从破产中艰难复苏的广为人知的故事，不过是近期的例子之一——古典乐迷和理论家们开始思索，这种艺术媒介该如何在不牺牲自身品格的前提下，融入21世纪的文化肌理。

words.meaning: adj. 高雅的；阳春白雪的

paragraphs.en: For example, should we feel OK "clapping" during classical music events, even if nobody else is? Why shouldn't we cheer for something great, like we do at a rock concert? The Huffington Post recently ran a Great Debate on this issue and many commenters came out on the side of silence.
paragraphs.zh: 例如，在古典音乐会上，哪怕旁人都没鼓掌，我们自己觉得精彩时“该不该鼓掌”？为什么我们不能像在摇滚音乐会上那样，为精彩的瞬间欢呼？《赫芬顿邮报》最近就此话题展开了一场大辩论，许多评论者都站在了保持沉默这一边。

paragraphs.en: "There is no more rewarding experience in life than being part of an audience where everybody is leaning forward in silence, thoroughly carried away by a great performance of a masterpiece," one commenter wrote. "Why is it so difficult for folks to develop an appreciation and understanding for the mannerisms and traditions of classical music?" asked another.
paragraphs.zh: 一位评论者写道：“人生中没有什么比成为这样的观众更充实的体验了：每个人都安静地前倾着身子，完全被一部杰作的伟大演绎所征服。”另一位则问道：“为什么人们就这么难去欣赏和理解古典音乐的风格与传统呢？”

paragraphs.en: The truth is that classical music audiences weren't always so polite. Robert Greenberg, an award-winning composer, said that when Beethoven first performed his 7th Symphony, audiences forced the orchestra to perform encores of certain movements immediately, applauding wildly. And in the last few decades, he said, many audiences at opera performances have abandoned pretenses, yelling "Bravo" when they feel like it.
paragraphs.zh: 真相是，古典音乐的观众并非一贯如此礼貌有加。获奖作曲家罗伯特·格林伯格说，贝多芬首次演奏其第七交响曲时，观众曾迫使乐团立即对某些乐章进行加演，并报以狂热的掌声。而在过去几十年里，许多歌剧观众也已放下矜持，会在情不自禁时高喊“好极了”。

paragraphs.en: "I don't think there's anything wrong with an audience showing their enthusiasm for a proper moment by applauding, showing their joy," Greenberg said, noting that the stuffiness in concert halls is "one aspect of contemporary concert etiquette" he doesn't understand. "Instead of waiting half an hour to show enthusiasm, why not show it every eight or nine minutes?"
paragraphs.zh: 格林伯格说：“我认为观众在适当的时刻用掌声表达热情、展现喜悦，这没什么不对。”他指出，音乐厅里那种沉闷拘谨的氛围是他无法理解的“当代音乐会礼仪的一个侧面”。“与其憋上半小时才表露热情，为什么不能每隔八九分钟就表达一次呢？”

paragraphs.en: Until the rules about behavior and clothing change, it's hard to imagine multitudes of young people filling concert halls on their own accord. They're probably more likely to head to Central Park to watch a free performance with a bottle of wine and their friends. "I think anyone should be able to come into a performance dressed any way they like, and be comfortable any way they like, sitting in that seat ready to enjoy themselves," Greenberg said.
paragraphs.zh: 在行为和着装规则改变之前，很难想象会有大批年轻人自发地坐满音乐厅。他们更可能的选择或许是带上一瓶酒，和朋友一起去中央公园看一场免费演出。格林伯格说：“我认为任何人都应该可以按自己喜欢的方式着装入场，以自己喜欢的方式舒服地坐在座位上，准备好享受音乐。因为这本身就是一件愉悦的事。”

paragraphs.en: Greenberg stressed that he doesn't want people to start respecting the music less, and he's not suggesting that we "dumb down" the experience. Rather, it's about opening up "access." When operas first instituted subtitles during shows, he said, many purists didn't like the idea, believing that the audience should instead study the works before attending.
paragraphs.zh: 格林伯格强调，他并非希望大家减少对音乐的尊重，也不是建议我们“降低”体验的门槛。相反，这是为了拓宽“准入”渠道。他说，歌剧刚开始在演出中设置字幕时，许多纯粹主义者并不赞同，他们认为观众理应事先做好功课。

paragraphs.en: But now it's commonplace to find titles on the seatback in front of you — choose a language, sit back, and understand what's going on. Allison Vulgamore, president of the Philadelphia Orchestra, is certainly looking to the future. She says certain "classics concerts" dedicated to the old masters will always exist, but not every program has to feature Beethoven and Brahms — or even a stage and seats.
paragraphs.zh: 但如今，在前座椅背上找到字幕屏已是司空见惯——选一种语言，安心靠坐，便能知晓台上正在发生什么。费城交响乐团团长艾莉森·瓦尔加莫无疑正着眼未来。她表示，致敬古典大师的特定“经典音乐会”将永远存在，但并非每场演出都必须以贝多芬和勃拉姆斯为核心——甚至都不一定要有传统的舞台和座位。

paragraphs.en: "We're trying to introduce different kinds of concerts in different ways," she said. "We are an interactive society now, where people like to learn." As the Philadelphia Orchestra rebounds from its financial straits, it is also aiming to experiment, without alienating the loyalists. Vulgamore pointed to Cirque de la Symphonie, a recent offering in which jugglers and acrobats interacted with musicians.
paragraphs.zh: 她说：“我们正尝试以不同的方式推出不同类型的音乐会。如今我们身处一个互动的社会，人们渴望学习新东西。”费城交响乐团在逐步走出财务困境的同时，也在努力尝试创新，同时避免疏远忠实乐迷。瓦尔加莫提到了近期推出的“交响马戏”，演出中杂耍演员和杂技演员与音乐家同台互动。

paragraphs.en: An upcoming collaboration with New York City's Ridge Theatre, meanwhile, will feature a "suspended dance installation" and other theatrical elements occurring in conjunction with an orchestral piece. The orchestra also continues to offer $25 annual memberships to Philadelphia students, who can buy rush tickets to every concert on the schedule.
paragraphs.zh: 同时，即将与纽约市里奇剧院合作的一场演出，将以“悬浮舞蹈装置”及其他戏剧元素与管弦乐作品交相辉映。乐团还继续向费城学生提供每年25美元的会员资格，学生会员可购买乐季中每一场音乐会的抢票。

paragraphs.en: "Students line up for the concerts they want, and we get roughly 300 or 350 kids a night coming to these. They take any of the open seats available, 5 minutes before the concert starts," Vulgamore said. "It's like the running of the bulls, that energy when the doors open."
paragraphs.zh: 瓦尔加莫说：“学生们会为自己想听的音乐会排队，每晚大约有300到350个孩子前来。音乐会开始前五分钟，他们会占据所有空余座位。那场面就像奔牛节一样，大门一开便充满能量。”

paragraphs.en: Greenberg thinks that youthful energy needs to be harvested. Conductors don't have to be arrogant and untouchable — they can be accessible. Perhaps there could even be a "bit of humor" about them, he suggested, and an abandoning of pretension within the high-art institutions themselves.
paragraphs.zh: 格林伯格认为，应当善加利用这股年轻活力。指挥家不必高高在上、不可接近——他们完全可以平易近人。他甚至建议，指挥家身上不妨带点“幽默感”，而高雅艺术机构自身也应放下架子。

paragraphs.en: "On one hand, these organizations are all saying the same thing: we want more general audiences, to break down cultural barriers," he said. "But then they come up with some very snooty thing that makes you crazy." John Terauds, a critic who has covered Toronto's classical music scene extensively, also wants to do away with the stuffiness.
paragraphs.zh: 他说：“一方面，这些机构都在说着同样的话：我们想要更多的普通观众，想打破文化壁垒。但转过头来，他们又做出一些傲慢至极、令人抓狂的事。”长期报道多伦多古典音乐界的评论家约翰·特罗兹也希望能消除这种沉闷感。

words.meaning: adj. 傲慢的；目中无人的

paragraphs.en: He suggested that the warmer an audience is, the better the musicians themselves will respond. "But the producer or organizer has to let everyone know it's OK," he said. "It's OK to enjoy yourself." At the Toronto Symphony Orchestra, for example, conductor Peter Oundjian often stops between pieces, taking a moment to talk about the composer or the music in a very amiable way.
paragraphs.zh: 他指出，观众越热情，音乐家自身的回应也会越好。“但制作人或组织者必须让所有人都知道，这是完全可以的，”他说，“享受其中，完全没问题。”例如在多伦多交响乐团，指挥彼得·翁吉安常常会在曲目间隙停下来，用非常亲切的方式花片刻时间谈谈作曲家或音乐本身。

paragraphs.en: And some nights, Terauds said, "at least a third" of the audience consists of students who have purchased cheaper tickets. On these nights, the energy of the room drastically shifts. It becomes a less intimidating place. Back in February, Terauds wrote on his blog about how going to classical performances can be intimidating.
paragraphs.zh: 特罗兹说，有些夜晚，“至少三分之一”的观众是购买了低价票的学生。在这些夜晚，场内的能量氛围会截然不同，音乐厅变得不再那么令人望而生畏。早在二月份，特罗兹就在博客中写道，去听古典音乐演出有时会让人心生怯意。

words.meaning: adj. 吓人的；令人胆怯的

paragraphs.en: Certain people "think they have to dress up," he wrote. "They think they have to know something about the music before they go. And, I'm sure, sitting in a seat, trembling in fear that this might be the wrong time to applaud, is also one of the factors." Everyone in the classical world agrees on the need for increased "accessibility," but achieving it is often easier said than done.
paragraphs.zh: 他写道，有些人“觉得自己必须盛装打扮，觉得去之前得对音乐有所了解。而且我敢肯定，坐在座位上担心此刻鼓掌是否合适而忐忑不安，也是原因之一。”古典音乐界人人都认同需要提高“可及性”，但知易行难。

paragraphs.en: Nowadays, there are unknown, unorthodox opera singers wowing viewers on TV programs like "America's Got Talent" and "The Voice". What can higher institutions do with any of that? And if they appeal to these outlets, do they risk compromising the integrity or the intelligence of the music? Vulgamore seems to understand this. She thinks an organization can have it both ways, claiming the new while keeping the old.
paragraphs.zh: 如今，一些名不见经传、风格另类的歌剧演员在《美国达人秀》和《美国好声音》等电视节目中大放异彩。高等艺术机构对此又能做些什么？如果它们迎合这类平台，是否又有损于音乐的品格与智识内涵？瓦尔加莫似乎深谙此道。她认为一个机构可以两面兼顾，在拥抱新事物的同时保留传统。

paragraphs.en: And as she reorganizes the Philadelphia Orchestra, she will attempt to do just that. "The world's most respected musicians brought together as an orchestra will always exist," she said. "But it's essential that we be willing to experiment and fail."
paragraphs.zh: 而在她重组费城交响乐团的过程中，她也正是打算这么做的。她说：“世界上最受尊敬的音乐家汇聚一堂组成的管弦乐团，将永远存在。但关键在于，我们必须愿意尝试并接受失败。”
